title: COVID-19
content: COVID-19 is a respiratory illness. Source: caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus, spread mainly through respiratory droplets and close contact with an infected person. Symptoms: fever, cough, fatigue, loss of taste or smell, sore throat, and shortness of breath in severe cases. Prevention: vaccination, wearing masks in crowded indoor spaces, good hand hygiene, and staying home when sick. Nutrition support: adequate protein for tissue repair, vitamin C and zinc for immune support, vitamin D, and staying well hydrated. Rest and medical care are essential; nutrition supports recovery but does not replace treatment.
keywords: COVID-19, COVID, coronavirus disease, SARS-CoV-2, respiratory illness, pandemic

title: Coronavirus
content: Coronaviruses are a family of viruses. Source: spread mainly via respiratory droplets and close contact; the family includes viruses causing the common cold as well as more severe diseases like SARS, MERS, and COVID-19 (caused by SARS-CoV-2). Symptoms: range from mild cold-like symptoms to severe respiratory illness depending on the specific virus. Prevention: hand hygiene, respiratory etiquette (covering coughs/sneezes), avoiding close contact with sick individuals, and vaccination where available. Nutrition support: adequate protein, vitamin C, vitamin D, and zinc, along with good hydration and rest, helps the body recover from viral respiratory infections.
keywords: Coronavirus, virus family, SARS, MERS, respiratory virus

title: Malaria
content: Malaria is a parasitic disease. Source: caused by Plasmodium parasites, transmitted through the bite of infected Anopheles mosquitoes (does not spread person-to-person through casual contact). Symptoms: high fever, chills, sweating, headache, nausea, and muscle pain, often occurring in cycles; can cause anemia due to destruction of red blood cells. Prevention: sleeping under insecticide-treated mosquito nets, using mosquito repellent, eliminating standing water where mosquitoes breed, and antimalarial preventive medication for travelers to high-risk areas. Nutrition support: iron and folate-rich foods to help rebuild red blood cells, plenty of fluids during fever, and easily digestible, calorie-dense foods during recovery. Antimalarial medical treatment is essential.
keywords: Malaria, Plasmodium, mosquito-borne disease, fever, anemia

title: Dengue Fever
content: Dengue fever is a viral infection. Source: spread by the bite of infected Aedes mosquitoes, which typically bite during the day and breed in stagnant water. Symptoms: sudden high fever, severe headache, pain behind the eyes, joint and muscle pain, rash, and a drop in platelet count; severe cases can lead to dengue hemorrhagic fever. Prevention: eliminating stagnant water around homes, using mosquito repellent and nets, and wearing long sleeves/pants in high-risk areas. Nutrition support: high fluid intake to prevent dehydration, vitamin C rich foods, and protein-rich foods to support platelet and tissue recovery; papaya leaf extract is a widely used traditional remedy, though medical monitoring of platelet counts is essential.
keywords: Dengue, dengue fever, Aedes mosquito, platelet count, viral fever

title: Chikungunya
content: Chikungunya is a viral infection closely related to dengue. Source: spread by the bite of infected Aedes mosquitoes. Symptoms: sudden fever and severe joint pain (often the most prominent symptom), muscle pain, headache, and rash; joint pain can persist for weeks or months after the fever resolves. Prevention: eliminating mosquito breeding sites (stagnant water), using mosquito repellent and nets, and wearing protective clothing. Nutrition support: anti-inflammatory foods (omega-3s, turmeric), adequate hydration, and rest during the acute fever phase.
keywords: Chikungunya, chickengunia, chikungunya fever, joint pain, Aedes mosquito

title: Asthma
content: Asthma is a chronic respiratory condition. Source: airways become inflamed and narrowed, triggered by allergens, cold air, exercise, respiratory infections, air pollution, or genetic predisposition (it is not contagious). Symptoms: wheezing, shortness of breath, chest tightness, and coughing. Prevention: identifying and avoiding personal triggers, using prescribed control medication consistently, getting flu/pneumonia vaccinations, and keeping indoor air clean. Nutrition support: an anti-inflammatory diet rich in omega-3 fatty acids, fruits, and vegetables; adequate vitamin D; and identifying/avoiding specific food triggers if present. Asthma requires ongoing medical management alongside supportive nutrition.
keywords: Asthma, wheezing, chronic respiratory disease, airway inflammation

title: CBC Report (Complete Blood Count)
content: A Complete Blood Count (CBC) is a common blood test. It measures red blood cells (RBC), white blood cells (WBC), hemoglobin, hematocrit (PCV), and platelets. Use: screens for and helps diagnose conditions like anemia (low hemoglobin/RBC), infections (high WBC), and clotting disorders (abnormal platelets). Prevention/monitoring: routine CBC testing helps catch nutrient deficiencies and blood disorders early, before symptoms become severe. Understanding your CBC report alongside a doctor's interpretation helps identify nutrient deficiencies (like iron or B12 deficiency) that may need dietary correction.
keywords: CBC, complete blood count, blood test, blood report, RBC, WBC, platelets, hemoglobin

title: Tuberculosis (TB)
content: Tuberculosis (TB) is a bacterial infection. Source: caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, primarily affecting the lungs, spread through airborne droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Symptoms: persistent cough (sometimes with blood), unexplained weight loss, night sweats, fever, and fatigue. Prevention: BCG vaccination (given in many countries at birth), good ventilation, early detection and treatment of active cases, and completing the full antibiotic course to prevent drug-resistant strains. Nutrition support: TB commonly causes malnutrition and significant weight loss, so a high-protein, high-calorie diet, along with adequate vitamin D and vitamin A, supports immune function and tissue repair during the long antibiotic treatment course.
keywords: Tuberculosis, TB, Mycobacterium tuberculosis, lung infection, persistent cough

title: Chickenpox
content: Chickenpox is a highly contagious viral infection. Source: caused by the varicella-zoster virus, spread through direct contact with the rash or through respiratory droplets; most common in children. Symptoms: itchy, blister-like rash, fever, tiredness, and loss of appetite. Prevention: varicella vaccination is highly effective; avoiding contact with infected individuals if not immune. Nutrition support: soft, easy-to-swallow foods if there are mouth sores, plenty of fluids to prevent dehydration, and vitamin C rich foods to support healing; avoid scratching to prevent scarring and secondary infection.
keywords: Chickenpox, chicken pox, varicella-zoster, viral rash

title: Jaundice
content: Jaundice is a yellowing of the skin and eyes caused by a buildup of bilirubin in the blood. Source: often signals an underlying liver problem (such as hepatitis), bile duct blockage, or excessive red blood cell breakdown, rather than being a disease itself. Prevention: vaccination against hepatitis, avoiding excess alcohol, maintaining a healthy weight to prevent fatty liver disease, and prompt treatment of underlying conditions. Nutrition support: a liver-friendly diet that's low in fat and avoids alcohol, adequate hydration, and easily digestible, high-fiber foods; the underlying cause needs medical diagnosis and treatment.
keywords: Jaundice, yellowing skin, bilirubin, liver problem

title: Food Poisoning
content: Food poisoning is an illness caused by consuming contaminated food or water. Source: bacteria (like Salmonella, E. coli), viruses, parasites, or their toxins in improperly handled, stored, or cooked food. Symptoms: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, stomach cramps, and sometimes fever. Prevention: thorough handwashing, cooking food to safe temperatures, avoiding cross-contamination, and proper refrigeration of perishables. Nutrition support: oral rehydration solution (ORS) or electrolyte fluids to prevent dehydration, a bland diet (bananas, rice, applesauce, toast) as symptoms ease, and probiotics to help restore healthy gut bacteria; seek medical care if symptoms are severe or persistent.
keywords: Food poisoning, foodborne illness, contaminated food, gastroenteritis

title: Hepatitis
content: Hepatitis is inflammation of the liver. Source: most often viral infections (Hepatitis A and E spread via contaminated food/water; Hepatitis B, C, and D spread via blood or bodily fluids), though it can also result from alcohol use, toxins, or autoimmune conditions. Symptoms: jaundice, fatigue, abdominal pain, nausea, and dark urine. Prevention: vaccination (available for Hepatitis A and B), safe food/water practices, avoiding sharing needles, and safe sex practices. Nutrition support: a balanced diet with adequate protein for liver repair, avoiding alcohol and fatty/fried foods, staying hydrated, and small frequent meals if appetite is reduced.
keywords: Hepatitis, hepatitis A, hepatitis B, hepatitis C, liver inflammation, viral hepatitis

title: Cholera
content: Cholera is a bacterial infection. Source: caused by Vibrio cholerae, usually spread through contaminated drinking water or food, especially where sanitation is poor. Symptoms: severe watery diarrhea and vomiting, which can lead to rapid, life-threatening dehydration. Prevention: access to clean drinking water, proper sanitation, food safety practices, and oral cholera vaccines in high-risk areas. Nutrition support: immediate oral rehydration solution (ORS) with the correct balance of salts and sugar is the most critical intervention, along with zinc supplementation, which has been shown to reduce the duration and severity of diarrhea.
keywords: Cholera, Vibrio cholerae, severe diarrhea, dehydration, waterborne disease

title: Diarrhea
content: Diarrhea is frequent, loose, or watery stools. Source: commonly caused by infections (viral, bacterial, or parasitic), food intolerances, contaminated water/food, or medications. Symptoms: can lead to dehydration and loss of electrolytes if prolonged. Prevention: safe drinking water, good hand hygiene, proper food handling, and avoiding known personal food triggers. Nutrition support: oral rehydration solution (ORS) or electrolyte drinks, the BRAT diet (bananas, rice, applesauce, toast), probiotics to restore gut bacteria, and temporarily avoiding dairy, fatty, and spicy foods until symptoms resolve.
keywords: Diarrhea, diarrhoea, loose stools, dehydration, gastrointestinal illness

title: Depression
content: Depression is a common mental health condition causing persistent sadness, loss of interest in activities, and difficulty with daily functioning. Source: arises from a combination of genetic, biological, environmental, and psychological factors (e.g. chronic stress, major life changes, trauma); it requires proper diagnosis and treatment by a mental health professional (therapy and/or medication). Prevention: maintaining social connections, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, stress management, and seeking help early when symptoms appear. Nutrition support: diets rich in omega-3 fatty acids, B vitamins, vitamin D, and probiotics (supporting the gut-brain axis) are associated with better mood regulation, but nutrition is a complement to professional treatment, not a replacement for it. If you or someone you know is struggling, please reach out to a healthcare provider.
keywords: Depression, mental health, mood disorder, gut-brain axis

title: Typhoid Fever
content: Typhoid fever is a bacterial infection. Source: caused by Salmonella Typhi, spread through food or water contaminated with the feces of an infected person. Symptoms: prolonged high fever, weakness, stomach pain, headache, and loss of appetite. Prevention: typhoid vaccination, safe drinking water, good hand hygiene, and proper sanitation. Nutrition support: a soft, high-calorie, easily digestible diet during recovery (khichdi-style meals, soups, bananas), adequate fluids, and small frequent meals since appetite is often reduced; antibiotic treatment is essential alongside nutritional support.
keywords: Typhoid, typhoid fever, Salmonella Typhi, prolonged fever

title: Flu (Influenza)
content: The flu (influenza) is a contagious viral respiratory infection. Source: spreads mainly through respiratory droplets when infected people cough, sneeze, or talk. Symptoms: fever, chills, muscle aches, cough, sore throat, congestion, and fatigue. Prevention: annual flu vaccination is the best prevention, along with good hand hygiene and avoiding close contact with sick individuals. Nutrition support: vitamin C and zinc rich foods to support immune function, warm fluids and soups (chicken soup has real anti-inflammatory and hydrating benefits), and adequate rest.
keywords: Flu, influenza, viral respiratory infection, seasonal flu

title: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus)
content: HIV is a virus that attacks the body's immune system, specifically CD4 (T-helper) cells. Source: spread through contact with certain body fluids of an infected person - unprotected sexual contact, sharing needles/syringes, mother-to-child transmission during pregnancy/birth/breastfeeding, or contaminated blood transfusions; it does not spread through casual contact, saliva, or air. Symptoms: flu-like illness within 2-4 weeks of infection (fever, fatigue, swollen lymph nodes), followed by a long asymptomatic period; without treatment, it progressively weakens the immune system. Prevention: consistent condom use, not sharing needles, pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) medication for high-risk individuals, regular testing, and antiretroviral treatment during pregnancy to prevent mother-to-child transmission. Nutrition support: a high-protein, adequate-calorie diet to maintain weight and muscle mass, micronutrient support (vitamin A, C, E, zinc, selenium) for immune function, and extra food safety care due to increased infection vulnerability.
keywords: HIV, human immunodeficiency virus, CD4 cells, immune system virus

title: AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome)
content: AIDS is the most advanced stage of HIV infection. Source: occurs when HIV has severely damaged the immune system (very low CD4 cell count) if left untreated, leaving the body vulnerable to opportunistic infections and certain cancers; the underlying cause is the same as HIV transmission (unprotected sex, needle sharing, mother-to-child transmission). Symptoms: rapid weight loss, recurring fever, prolonged swollen lymph glands, chronic diarrhea, persistent sores, pneumonia, and memory problems, largely from opportunistic infections the weakened immune system can no longer fight off. Prevention: the same prevention measures as HIV, plus - most importantly - early HIV diagnosis and consistent antiretroviral therapy (ART), which prevents progression from HIV to AIDS almost entirely. Nutrition support: high-protein, high-calorie diet with micronutrient support (vitamin A, C, E, zinc, selenium), strict food safety (avoiding raw/undercooked foods) due to severely weakened immunity, and small frequent meals if appetite is poor.
keywords: AIDS, acquired immunodeficiency syndrome, advanced HIV, opportunistic infections

title: Cancer
content: Cancer is a group of diseases involving abnormal, uncontrolled cell growth that can invade nearby tissue and spread to other parts of the body. Source: caused by genetic mutations in cells, driven by risk factors including tobacco use, excessive alcohol, poor diet, obesity, physical inactivity, UV/radiation exposure, certain infections (e.g. HPV, Hepatitis B/C), family history, and environmental carcinogens. Symptoms: vary widely by type, but general warning signs include unexplained weight loss, persistent fatigue, unusual lumps, changes in skin, a persistent cough, and changes in bowel/bladder habits. Prevention: avoiding tobacco, limiting alcohol, maintaining a healthy weight, regular physical activity, a balanced diet rich in fruits/vegetables/fiber, limiting processed and red meat, protecting skin from UV exposure, HPV and Hepatitis B vaccination, and regular screenings for early detection. Nutrition support: antioxidant-rich fruits and vegetables, adequate protein (especially important during treatment), fiber for digestive/colon health, and limiting processed meats and excess alcohol; nutrition can support prevention and recovery but medical treatment is essential.
keywords: Cancer, tumor, malignant cells, oncology, carcinogen
